title: Persistent memory contamination across user sessions
summary: Unverified, attacker-influenced, or incorrectly attributed information is stored as durable memory and later shapes decisions in a different task, session, user context, or workspace.
trust_boundaries: stored memory to future context assembly
preconditions: The system stores summaries, preferences, instructions, facts, or plans beyond the current interaction.
Memory writes can be influenced by retrieved content, tool output, or model-generated summaries.
Future tasks automatically retrieve memory without a strong provenance or ownership check.
The system does not reliably expire, review, or isolate action-shaping memory.
attack_path: A legitimate task exposes the system to misleading or instruction-like external content.
The system compresses the interaction into a memory item without preserving enough source context.
The memory item is stored as a trusted preference, fact, rule, contact, or operating assumption.
A later task retrieves the item and treats it as established context.
The later plan or action is changed even though the current user did not provide or approve the stored instruction.
observable_signals: A later action cites a rule or preference that cannot be traced to the current user request.
A memory item lacks source, owner, creation time, confidence, or expiry metadata.
Content from one user, tenant, or workspace appears in another context.
The same misleading fact continues to affect behavior after the original source is removed.
Deleting the visible conversation does not remove the action-shaping state derived from it.
limitations: Not every incorrect memory item is malicious; ordinary summarization and attribution errors can create the same symptoms.
Useful long-term personalization may require retaining uncertain information with clear labels rather than deleting it.
Memory behavior differs across application-managed stores, model context, caches, files, and external services.
A provenance record does not guarantee that the source itself was truthful or authorized.
open_questions: Which memory classes should require explicit confirmation before they influence tool use?
How can provenance survive summarization without storing unnecessary personal data?
What deletion semantics are needed when a memory item has already influenced downstream state?
How should systems test tenant isolation and ownership when memory is retrieved semantically?
